title: TesTly on Your Phone
subtitle: One app for iPhone and Android
intro: TesTly is also a phone app, not just a website. Patients and lab staff can do almost everything from their pocket.
bullets: Built once with Flutter, runs on both iPhone and Android
Talks to the same backend server as the website
Search labs, book tests, see results, and chat with the AI helper

title: One App, Two Phones
intro: Instead of writing two separate apps, we wrote one and it runs everywhere.
imageCaption: The phone app is one of two front doors to the same backend server.
bullets: We write the app a single time using a tool called Flutter
Flutter turns that one project into a real iPhone app AND a real Android app
This saves a huge amount of time and keeps both phones working the same way
The phone app and the website share the exact same backend, so the data is always in sync

title: Tech Stack & Why
intro: The main tools we used to build the phone app, and the reason for each.

cards.name: Flutter & Dart
cards.what: Flutter is a toolkit for building phone apps; Dart is the programming language it uses.
cards.why: We write the app once and Flutter makes both the iPhone and Android versions, so we build twice as fast.

cards.name: Riverpod
cards.what: It holds the app's live data (like your bookings) and keeps every screen showing the latest version.
cards.why: When data changes, only the screens that need it refresh, so the app stays fast and never shows stale info.

cards.name: Dio
cards.what: The messenger that sends requests to the backend server and brings back the answers.
cards.why: It is reliable, handles timeouts, and lets us attach your login token to every request in one central place.

cards.name: go_router
cards.what: Decides which screen you see and moves you between screens (login, home, results, etc.).
cards.why: It can automatically send you to the right place based on whether you are logged in and your role.

cards.name: fl_chart
cards.what: Draws the line graphs that show how your health numbers change over time.
cards.why: It is flexible enough to color bad readings red and draw the safe range, making trends easy to read.

cards.name: flutter_secure_storage
cards.what: A locked safe inside the phone for storing secret data like your login token.
cards.why: It uses the phone's built-in hardware vault, so your login stays safe even if the phone is lost.

title: Key Libraries & Why
intro: Extra ready-made building blocks we added so we didn't have to build everything from scratch.

rows.name: firebase_messaging
rows.what: Receives push notifications from Google's servers, like 'Your result is ready'.
rows.why: It lets the app alert you even when it is closed, so you never miss an update.

rows.name: google_maps_flutter
rows.what: Shows real Google Maps inside the app to display lab locations.
rows.why: Maps are the clearest way to show where a lab is; we use Google's proven map instead of drawing our own.

rows.name: geolocator
rows.what: Asks the phone for your current location (with your permission).
rows.why: Knowing where you are lets the app find and sort the nearest labs to you.

rows.name: pdfx
rows.what: Opens and displays your result PDF document right inside the app.
rows.why: You can read your official result without leaving the app or downloading a separate file.

rows.name: share_plus
rows.what: Opens the phone's normal share menu to send a result to someone (e.g. your doctor).
rows.why: It reuses the phone's built-in sharing, so it feels natural and works with any app.

rows.name: local_auth
rows.what: Unlocks the app using Face ID or your fingerprint.
rows.why: It adds a quick, private lock so only you can open your health data.

title: How the App Is Organized: 3 Simple Layers
intro: We split the code into three tidy layers so it stays easy to understand and fix.
imageCaption: Screens you see, the logic in the middle, and the data from the server.
bullets: Layer 1 - What you see: the buttons, lists, and screens you tap on
Layer 2 - The logic & state: keeps track of data and decides what happens next
Layer 3 - The data: talks to the backend server and brings information in and out
Each layer only talks to its neighbor, so a screen never sends a network request directly - this keeps things clean and easy to test

title: How the App Remembers Your Login Safely
intro: After you log in once, you should not have to type your password every time. Here is how we keep you logged in without risking your account.

steps.title: Step 1: You log in
steps.detail: You type your email and password once. The backend checks them and sends back two secret keys: an access token and a refresh token.

steps.title: Step 2: Keys go into the phone's vault
steps.detail: We save those keys in flutter_secure_storage, which is the iPhone Keychain or the Android Keystore - a hardware-protected safe. Plain settings never hold secrets.

steps.title: Step 3: Every request carries the key
steps.detail: From then on, Dio quietly attaches the access token to each request to the server, so the server knows it is really you.

steps.title: Step 4: Logout clears the vault
steps.detail: When you sign out, the keys are wiped from the safe and the app forgets you, so nobody else can use your session.

title: How the App Talks to the Backend
intro: Every time you open a screen, the app fetches fresh data from the server. Here is the round trip.
imageCaption: The phone asks, the server answers, the screen updates.

steps.title: Step 1: The screen asks for data
steps.detail: For example the Bookings screen asks: 'give me this patient's bookings and results.' It asks through Riverpod, not directly.

steps.title: Step 2: Dio sends the request with your token
steps.detail: Dio packages the request, attaches your access token so the server trusts it, and sends it over the internet to the backend.

steps.title: Step 3: The server replies with JSON
steps.detail: The backend sends back the data in a simple text format called JSON. The app turns that text into neat Dart objects it can use.

steps.title: Step 4: Riverpod shows it and remembers it
steps.detail: Riverpod hands the data to the screen and caches it, so going back to that screen is instant instead of asking the server again.

title: Maps & Finding Nearby Labs
intro: When you search for a test, the app can show which labs are closest to you.

steps.title: Step 1: Ask for your location
steps.detail: Using geolocator, the app politely asks permission to know where you are. If you say no, it simply skips the distance sorting instead of breaking.

steps.title: Step 2: Measure the distance to each lab
steps.detail: The app knows each lab's coordinates. It calculates the straight-line distance from you to every lab using a formula called haversine.

steps.title: Step 3: Sort nearest first
steps.detail: Labs are reordered so the closest one is at the top, and each shows a friendly 'X km away' label.

steps.title: Step 4: Show it on Google Maps
steps.detail: Lab locations can be displayed on a real Google Map (via google_maps_flutter), the clearest way to see exactly where to go.

title: How Push Notifications Work
intro: The app can tap you on the shoulder with news like 'Your result is ready' even when it is closed.

steps.title: Step 1: The app gets a delivery address
steps.detail: On login, Firebase gives the phone a unique token - like a mailbox address. The app sends this address to our backend so it knows where to reach you.

steps.title: Step 2: Something happens on the server
steps.detail: A lab uploads your result, or confirms your booking. The backend decides you should be told.

steps.title: Step 3: Firebase delivers the message
steps.detail: The backend asks Firebase Cloud Messaging to deliver a notification to your phone's mailbox address. It arrives even if the app is shut.

steps.title: Step 4: Tapping it opens the right screen
steps.detail: When you tap the notification, the app opens straight to that booking or result, instead of dumping you on the home screen.

title: Clever Bit: Automatic Token Refresh
intro: Your access token expires after a while for safety. Without a fix, you would suddenly get logged out mid-action. We make the app quietly fix this by itself.

steps.title: Notice the expired key
steps.detail: When a request comes back saying 'not allowed' (error 401), the app realizes the access token has expired.

steps.title: Quietly ask for a new one
steps.detail: It pulls out the longer-lived refresh token from the secure vault and asks the server for a brand-new access token - all in the background.

steps.title: Save and retry
steps.detail: It stores the fresh token in the vault, then automatically retries the original request. You never noticed anything happened.

steps.title: Handle a crowd safely
steps.detail: If several requests fail at the same moment, a queue makes sure they don't all rush to refresh at once - only one refresh happens, then everyone retries.

why: This keeps you logged in smoothly for a long time while still expiring tokens often for security - you get safety without the annoyance.

title: Clever Bit: Drawing Health-Trend Charts
intro: We turn a list of past test results into an easy-to-read line graph so you can spot if a number is going up, down, or staying steady.

steps.title: Gather the readings
steps.detail: The app collects each past reading for a marker (like blood sugar) with its date and value, already made comparable across different labs by the backend.

steps.title: Plot the line with fl_chart
steps.detail: fl_chart draws a line connecting the points over time, with the date along the bottom and the value up the side.

steps.title: Highlight what matters
steps.detail: Readings outside the safe range are colored red, and dashed lines show the healthy low and high limits, so problems jump out instantly.

steps.title: Add a plain-English badge
steps.detail: A small badge says 'rising', 'falling', or 'stable' with a short sentence, so you understand the trend even without reading the graph.

why: A single PDF can't show change over time; this chart lets a patient actually see their health trend at a glance across many tests and labs.

title: Clever Bit: Keeping Screens in Sync with Riverpod
intro: When data changes in one place, every screen that shows it should update automatically - without us writing tricky code to refresh each one by hand.

steps.title: Fetch once, store centrally
steps.detail: The patient's bookings, results, and profile are fetched in a single call and kept in one shared place called a provider.

steps.title: Each screen watches its slice
steps.detail: A screen 'watches' only the part it cares about - for example the Results tab watches just the results, not the whole bundle.

steps.title: Change once, update everywhere
steps.detail: When the data updates, Riverpod automatically rebuilds only the screens watching that slice. Untouched screens are left alone.

steps.title: Pull to refresh
steps.detail: Swiping down tells Riverpod to throw away the cached data and fetch fresh data, and the screen updates itself the moment it arrives.

why: This makes the app feel alive and consistent - you never see one screen disagree with another - while keeping the code simple and fast.

title: Code: Distance Calculation in Dart
intro: The mobile app calculates lab distances locally on the phone — identical formula to the backend and website so every client shows the same number.
note: **`LocationAccuracy.low`** is intentional — we only need a city-level fix to sort labs by distance, not precision navigation. `low` accuracy is 5–10× faster to obtain and uses far less battery. If the user denies permission or the timeout fires, `getCurrentLocation()` returns **null** and the app falls back to sorting by rating instead of distance, gracefully.

title: Code: Automatic Token Refresh
intro: When an API call returns '401 Unauthorized' (access token expired), the Dio interceptor silently fetches a new token and retries — the user never gets logged out mid-action.
note: The `_isRefreshing` flag + `_queue` pattern is critical: if 5 API calls fail at the same moment, only **one** refresh request is sent. The other 4 are queued and retried with the new token once the refresh completes. Without this, 5 simultaneous refresh calls would flood the server and likely all fail, logging the user out.

title: Q&A: Mobile Deep Questions
intro: Questions about Flutter architecture, security, and how the app handles real-world conditions.

pairs.q: Why Flutter instead of React Native?
pairs.a: Flutter uses its own rendering engine (Skia/Impeller) to draw every pixel — it does not use native UI components. This means **pixel-perfect UI on both platforms** with no native bridge slowdowns. Dart compiles to native ARM code. React Native calls native components through a JS bridge, which can cause frame drops. Flutter is also backed by Google, which matters for long-term support.

pairs.q: Why Riverpod instead of Provider or Bloc?
pairs.a: Riverpod is a compile-time-safe version of Provider that does not depend on the widget tree. Providers are defined globally, can be read from anywhere, and are automatically disposed when no longer watched. Bloc is more verbose (requires events, states, and a bloc class). For a team project, Riverpod's **simpler API and better testability** made it the right choice.

pairs.q: What is flutter_secure_storage and why not just SharedPreferences?
pairs.a: `SharedPreferences` writes data to a plain text file on disk — anyone who can read the device storage (e.g. via a backup on a rooted phone) can see the tokens. `flutter_secure_storage` uses **iOS Keychain** (hardware-encrypted) and **Android Keystore** (hardware-backed encryption chip). A login token stolen from Keychain is cryptographically unusable without the device's secure enclave.

pairs.q: How does the app handle a bad internet connection?
pairs.a: Dio is configured with a connection timeout and a receive timeout. If the server does not respond in time, Dio throws a `DioException` with type `connectionTimeout` or `receiveTimeout`. The app catches this in each screen's `AsyncValue` handler and shows an error state with a 'Retry' button. Riverpod's `ref.refresh()` re-fetches the data on tap — no need to restart the app.

pairs.q: How does tapping a push notification open the right screen?
pairs.a: Firebase Cloud Messaging delivers the notification with a **data payload** that includes a `type` ('booking', 'result') and an `id`. When the user taps it, `firebase_messaging` calls our `onMessageOpenedApp` handler. We read the payload and call `GoRouter`'s `push()` with the matching route (e.g. `/patient/results/abc123`). If the app is closed, `getInitialMessage()` retrieves the payload on first launch.

title: Putting It All Together
intro: Here is the whole phone app in one picture: one codebase, three layers, talking safely to one backend.
imageCaption: Everything a patient can do from their phone, powered by the shared backend.
bullets: One Flutter app gives us both iPhone and Android - less work, perfect parity
Three clean layers keep the code tidy: screens, logic, and data
Dio plus a secure vault keep you logged in safely with automatic token refresh
Maps, location, charts, and push notifications make it a full, helpful health companion
Because it shares the backend with the website, your data is always the same everywhere
